source_metadata.title_raw: Ron Criss - A-82-029 - February 8, 1982 - Central Valley

content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660         322-5901         322-5441         322-5644

February 8, 1982


Ron Criss
Acting General Manager
Shasta Dam Area Public Utility
District
1650 Stanton Drive
Central Valley, CA  96019

Dear Mr. Criss:

     Thank you for sending the information that I received
January 22, 1982; this material has answered the remaining
questions I had.  As you know, your predecessor, Mr. Michael
Kenny, asked the Fair Political Practices Commission whether
the Shasta Dam Public Utility District (District) must include
Mr. W. A. Gelonek, who performs professional engineering
services for the District, as a designated consultant in its
Conflict of Interest Code.  You have indicated a continuing
interest in this question.

     As I understand the situation, during the last year,1/
Mr. Gelonek and his firm, Gelonek and Associates, have performed
work for the District under two types of contracts.  In the
majority of cases, the firm was employed to work on specific
engineering projects, such as the preparation of a report or
study of the design of a particular District sewage treatment
plant or other project.  (Examples of this are Contract
#8730, "Sewage Treatment Plant Expansion," and Contract
#8028, "Continundi Site Study.")  The other type of contract
entered into was for "General Engineering Services."

     Under the contracts labeled "General Engineering Services"
(Contracts #8101 and 8002), Mr. Gelonek appears to have
performed the type of activities that would be performed by

_______________________________
1/ For purposes of this letter, I will focus on the work
Mr. Gelonek performed for the District during 1980-1981,
although I am aware that he performed engineering services
for the District prior to this time.

Ron Criss
February 8, 1982
Page Two


a full-time staff engineer in a larger district: he reviewed
and updated the time schedule for District projects; he
participated in discussions regarding District personnel and
needs (specifically the review of the Engineering Tech I
position needs of the District); he was involved in the
preliminary estimates and design of possible future projects;
he prepared and presented reports to the Board; he prepared
letters on sewer requirements; and he met with District
personnel to discuss technical matters.

The initial question we were asked was whether the
position of engineering consultant must be designated in the
District's Conflict of Interest Code. The Political Reform
Act requires consultants to local governmental entities to
be designated in the entity's code if the consultant makes
or participates in the making of decisions which may foreseeably
have a material effect on any financial interest. (Government
Code Section 87302.2/ copy enclosed.)

A "consultant," within the meaning of the Act, includes
"any natural person who provides, under contract, information,
advice, recommendation or counsel to a local government
agency ..." (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(2), copy
enclosed). This term does not include, however, a person
who:

(A) Conducts research and arrives at conclusions
with respect to his or her rendition of information,
advice, recommendation or counsel independent of the
control and direction of the agency or any agency official,
other than normal contract monitoring; and

(B) Possesses no authority with respect to any
agency decision beyond the rendition of information,
advice, recommendation or counsel.

2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18700(a)(2)(A)
and (B).

When an engineer is hired under a contract to perform
design or other discrete engineering services for a specific
project or study, he ordinarily comes within this exemption.
Under such circumstance, the engineer uses his own judgment

2/Hereinafter all statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise specified.

Ron Criss
February 8, 1982
Page Three


and expertise to render professional services according to
the specifications of a contract, and his decisions are not
subject to day-to-day review or direction by the District.
Essentially, the engineer is being called upon to deliver a
finished product -- a report, or a design -- and not to
participate in or advise the District on general governmental
decisions requiring engineering expertise.2/  Applying this
analysis to Mr. Gelonek's situation, when he performs under
the types of contracts for specific projects which have been
awarded to him in the past, he does not appear to be the
type of consultant covered by the Act.

     On the other hand, when an engineer performs the type
of activities rendered by Mr. Gelonek under the "General
Engineering Services" contract, it is our view that he is a
consultant within the meaning of the Act.  The day-to-day
contact with district officials and the fact that he is
filling the role of District Engineer indicate that he does
not act with the independence necessary to trigger the exemption.
He is not delivering a finished product such as an EIR report
or a design for a specific project, but is entering into the
daily decision-making process of the District.  In performing
these duties, he does not merely offer the District his
professional opinion on specific questions, but appears to
participate in meetings and discussions where he plays a key
role in the actual decision-making process.

     For these reasons, when Mr. Gelonek acts under the
"General Engineering Services" contract he is a consultant
within the meaning of the Act.  The decisions in which he
participates as a consultant appear to be the sort of decision
that may foreseeably have a material effect on a financial
interest.2/  Thus his position must be designated in the
District's Conflict of Interests Code, and proper disclosure

_______________
     2/This would be true even if some incidental advice
about the specific project were provided as part of these
professional services.  For example, the engineer could advise
the District on what materials should be used for construction,
or that the project could not be constructed within the proposed
budget.

     4/An example of this is that the consulting engineer
might participate in a decision on whether to construct sewer
lines to a previously undeveloped area.  Such a decision
would foreseeably have a material effect on real property in
this area.

Ron Criss
February 8, 1982
Page Four


categories must be developed for this position. In determining
what disclosure is appropriate for Mr. Gelonek you may want
to consider the types of investments, real property interests
and sources of income that other District officials are
required to disclose.

As a consultant who should be designated in the District's
Code, Mr. Gelonek is subject to the disqualification provisions
of the Political Reform Act when he performs services under
the "General Engineering Services" contract. However, Mr. Gelonek
is not subject to the disqualification provisions of the Act
with respect to services performed under contracts for specific
projects such as those which you have provided. In connection
with the General Engineering Services contract, Mr. Gelonek
may not make, participate in making or in any way attempt to
use his official position to influence a government decision
in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest. (Section 87100, copy enclosed.) He has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect on the
type of interests specified in Section 87103 (copy enclosed).
In determining whether a financial effect is "material,"
guidance may be obtained from 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702
(copy enclosed).

There are several types of decisions in which Mr. Gelonek
may be called upon to participate under the General Engineering
Services contract which may call for disqualification. For
example, he may review the District's overall timetable for
its projects, or may help the District determine whether
work on a particular project meets the District's specifications.
He may make or participate in decisions of this type so long
as he is not being called upon to review the specific projects
for which he has been hired as the engineer under a separate
contract. He will have to disqualify himself from decisions
affecting those specific projects if the decisions can materially
financially affect him or his business. See the Maloney
opinion, 3 FPPC Opinions 69 (copy enclosed).

Mr. Gelonek may also be called upon to give general
advice to the District concerning the need for new engineering
projects. Because Mr. Gelonek is awarded a large number of
contracts for specific projects by the district, it may well
be foreseeable that a District decision to undertake a specific
project will result in Mr. Gelonek's obtaining the contract
for that project. Since the award of such an engineering

Pcn Criss
February 8, 1982
Page Five


contract will clearly have a material financial effect on
Mr. Gelonek, he must disqualify himself from decisions which
may foreseeably result in his receiving such a contract.
However, if it is not foreseeable that Mr. Gelonek will be
awarded the contract, he may participate in decisions relating
to a future project.

     Under the District's present system of awarding engineering
contracts, as you described it, we would have to conclude
that it is foreseeable that Mr. Gelonek will be awarded certain
types of contracts. The District's current procedure, as I
understand it, is first to approach an engineer who it knows
has expertise in the field, and who has done similar work for
the District in the past, to try to negotiate a contract.
Looking at the past history of the District, it is foreseeable
that, at least for projects within Mr. Gelonek's area of
expertise, he is the engineer who is most likely to be offered
the opportunity to do the engineering work. Thus, as long as
the District continues to use this system of awarding engineering
contracts, Mr. Gelonek should disqualify himself from participating
in decisions regarding future projects (or new work on or
renovation of projects completed in the past) of the type for
which he has been hired in the past to perform the engineering
services.

     I would like to point out, however, that there are other
possible methods for hiring engineers for specific District
projects, which, if used, may remove the need for Mr. Gelonek's
disqualification on decisions regarding certain future projects.
Some of these methods may make it unforeseeable that Mr. Gelonek
would be the engineer who would receive the contract for a
specific project. For example, the District could advertise
its need for an engineer for a specific project, invite applications
from engineers within a large enough area to ensure that
several qualified firms might apply, and select the project
engineer from among the applicants by some open and objective
process.     If such a process were used, and if Mr. Gelonek
did not automatically receive most or all the contracts within
his area of expertise under such a system, it would no longer
be foreseeable at the planning stages that Mr. Gelonek would
obtain the final contract for a project. Thus Mr. Gelonek

___________________________

5/Of course, Mr. Gelonek could not participate in this
selection process if he were one of the applicants.

Ron Criss
February 8, 1982
Page Six


would not have to disqualify himself from the planning decisions
in which he now participates under the General Engineering
Services contract.5/

In conclusion, I would like to state that this advice
letter concerns only the application of the Political Reform
Act. You should consult the District's attorney as to the
possible application of any other laws.

If you have any questions, please feel free to contact
me at (916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Janis Shank McLean
Counsel
Legal Division

JSM:plh
Enclosures

_________________________

5/Under such a system, Mr. Gelonek would still be subject
to the disclosure requirements of the Act.

Box 793 Project C[?]r
Calif. 96079.
Nov. 16, 1981.

[illegible] O 55 an Ul

California Fair Political Practices Comm.          In re: W.A. Gelonic
P.O. Box 807, Sacramento, 95804.                 Shasta Dam Area Public Utility
                                                  District.
Dear Ms. J. L. Shark:

This Committee, "Citizens for Reform" Shasta Dam Area Public Utility
District, the three elected Directors, and residents, were puzzled and
shocked by uncovering your letter of October 27, 1981 to the Manager of
this District, found buried in notices on bulletin board in meeting room
and by the later uncovered office correspondence of this Manager to your
Commission dated October 9, and 16, 1981. as you have made no attempt
whatsoever to cover any other concerned or active residents on this subject.

We request an explanation.

His letter of October 16, 1981 to a Ms. Barbara Milman states,
par. 3, "An section will be conducted on Nov. 3 to elect 3 members to the
Board of Directors and this issue has become a political issue within the
community."

This statement by this Manager is totally false.

At no time in any manner was this a political or any other issue in this
campaign for Directors. In no news items, political advertisements, in any
newspapers was any Gelonic issue or name ever remotely mentioned by anyone.
Nor in any distributed, circulated, political campaign literature ever
remotely referred to. The sole public forum arranged by the League of
Woman Voters attended by of 11 candidates in statements, questions and
answers, no mention by anyone was ever made. Our recent survey of a cross
section of 50 voters reveals none ever heard of it and few of Gelonic at all.

This Committee, candidates, and residents are are puzzle d by this
recently uncovered serial, motives and requests of this Manager, and the
obviously secret investigation, and "preparing an answer" without any
attempt at contact to those vitally concerned and well identified in public
news print and public records as so concerned, and possessing vital data,
records, material, as a result of surveys, study, and investigation.

We demand a full explanation.

The Manager's ambitious letter dated October 9, page 2, par. 2, states,
"Those concerned with the District wish to resolve this matter of conflict of
interest and base tabled to the matter so concerned..." This is false.
This manager has never tabled with this writer, publicly identified in
n ers, and meetings, and minutes thereof, or others so concerned.

As historical background of W. A. Gelonic conflict of interest.
Last summer, records available, the subject was brought up in open meeting,
as a legal not political concern. Later a vague letter from the attorney
of the District was revealed with little opinion and suggesting more facts.
This is being complied by "those concerned." No known information has been
relayed by anyone to date. With the completion of the brief it is our
intention, Committee, new Directors, concerned citizens to request a
Legislative inquiry into this matter to determine if the laws are being
observed, and if so, the introduction of remedial legislation for the purpose
of correcting the evil.

In re Gelomic contract No. 8101 sent by this Manager, the term
"General Engineering Services" is totally misleading, incomplete, and
deficient as a description of services performed.

Completely omitted is any mention of all services performed, by
Gelomic for which the firm has been paid many, many, thousands of dollars,
completely removed from the purview of "General Engineering Services"
such as remote advice, and urging EIRS containing blatant, erronous,
conclusions, unsubstantiated, and unproven, and well contrived to
influence government decisions.

This Committee, with the concurrence of the majority of the 3 newly
elected Directors, requests from your Commission under the California
Public Disclosure Act the following information:

1. Copy of your complete file on this previously undisclosed, subject,
including all correspondence, results and notations of telephone
conversations, etc.

2. Reports of any investigators, such as Mark D. Walker,
and his expenses on this investigation.

3. Names of Chairman of this Commission and members, not given for
some reason on your letter heads.

This Committee, and from others, I am instructed to inform you that
the suspicion is well founded, and the 12,000 residents of this District
who voted 8 to 1 in a recent runoff against the policies and practices
of the management of this District and of the 3 removed Directors, will not
sit still for evasion of this Commission with red tape taken to obtain
conspicuous data, evidence, material, conspicuous, and readily available
at all times. Unless a full explanation is given the present evidence is
of a whitewash, coverup, snow job, with what motivation now only suspect.
This is a subject of Legislative inquiry. Your investigation is totally
deficient.

William Coyle, coChairman, "Citizens for
Reform," S.D.A. P.U.D./

Retired Member,
California State Democratic Central Committee
Five Counties Central Labor Council
CDC, COPE, etc.

cc:                                                  "Citizens for Reform"  SDAPUD,
Hon. Ray Johnson                           
California State Senate                    Board of Directors, Shasta Dam
                                          Area Public Utility District.
Hon. Stan Strathan
California State Assembly

Franklin Cibula, Attorney at Law.
Patrick Coyle, Attorney at Law.

P. O. Box 777                                                   Telephone 275-8827


                    Shasta Dam Area Public Utility District
                              1650 Stanton Drive
                        CENTRAL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 96019

                              October 16, 1981




Ms Barbara Milman
Executive/Legal Division
State of California
' Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, Ca 95804

Re:  GC-81/0706

Dear Ms Milman:

     The enclosed material is submitted to your office in
accordance with a suggestion from Mark D. Walker,
Investigator II, of Enforcement Division.

     It is our understanding that your office will render
an opinion as to the possibility of a conflict of interest
re services performed by the Firm of W. A. Gelonek and
Affiliates for the District.

     I will appreciate receiving your findings in this matter
as soon as possible. An election will be conducted on
November 3 to elect three members of the Board of Directors
and this issue has become a political issue within the community.

     Thank you for your interest,

     Sincerely,

     [signature]

     Michael B. Kenny,
     General Manager

     MBK: mp

     enc

     cc: Mark D. Walker
         Investigator II
         Enforcement Division

W. A. GELONEK & AFFILIATES INC.
Planning and Engineering for a Quality Environment


October 15, 1981


Michael B. Kenny, General Manager
SHASTA DAM AREA PUBLIC UTILITY DISTRICT
1650 Stanton Drive
Central Valley, California 96019

Re: Scope of Services of W. A. Gelonek & Affiliates, Inc.

Dear Mr. Kenny:

     This letter summarizes the services offered by our firm
in our capacity as Civil Engineers and Planners. The attached
list is general in nature and is intended to describe the breadth
of our past services.

     It is our practice to be engaged by a client on a project
by project basis for specific services within our field of
expertise.

     In addition, and in accordance with your needs, we can
help you with:

     a. Assistance in Water Supply Contract negotiations
     b. Special maps and aerial photographs preparation
     c. Review of District utility standards, i.e.
        minimum pipe size determination etc.
     d. Land surveys for easements
     e. Legal descriptions for easements
     f. Engineering estimates for updating facility
        capacity fees
     g. Assistance in determining utility needs for
        proposed commercial and residential subdivisions

     We have been providing personal services to Special Districts
for over 16 years in Northern California and look forward to
assisting the Shasta Dam Area Public Utility District in any way
we can.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        W. A. GELONEK & AFFILIATES, INC.

                                        [signature]
                                        W. A. Gelonek

P. O. Box 777                                                     Telephone 275-8827


                    Shasta Dam Area Public Utility District
                                1650 Stanton Drive
                        CENTRAL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 96019

                                October 9, 1981


Fair Political Practice Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, Ca 95814

Re: Conflict, District/Engineering Firm
    W. A. Gelonek and Affiliates

Att: Mr. Mark Walker

Gentlemen:

This is written in response to our phone conversation of
October 8, 1981, and Mr. Walker's inquiry about engineering
services provided to the District by the Firm of W. A. Gelonek,
under the terms of Contract No. 8101, a copy of which is provided
herewith for your review.

The District's procedure has been to budget, each year, funds for
professional and contractual services, including legal, financial
and auditing, engineering and design, and certain special services
such as recreational instruction, janitorial and tree trimming, etc.

Expenditure of these funds, which have been authorized by the Board
of Directors, is approved by the General Manager, for services
which have been negotiated, contracted, or which have been
recommended to him by District Department Heads who have solicited
the price by bid, inquiry or contract proposal. All service agree-
ments, of whatever nature, are finally approved by the Board of
Directors, in accordance with funds which have been budgeted for
that purpose. If services are used in addition to the services
provided in the budget, the additional appropriation must be
approved by appropriate action of the Board of Directors.

The District does not, as a practice, solicit bids each year for the
legal and engineering services, but has contracted with various
firms, with no consulting or retainer fees paid, with compensation
paid in accordance with their current schedule of charges, for
services performed during the fiscal year.

Generally, the Engineer or Attorney who is familiar with the
District's background, projects or issues is selected, with such
experience helping to reduce the District's costs. Fair and
equitable competition is difficult between sources totally
uninformed about the District and those having much background
and experience with the District's affairs.

Fair Political Practice Commission
October 9, 1981                                          Page 2


In the past, when projects or cases have been completed,
generally a different firm has been selected, based on
preferences for the types of work to be performed, or services
rendered.

Those concerned with the District wish to resolve this matter
of "Conflict of Interest", and have talked with the persons
so concerned, but have so far been unable to resolve at the
District level.

I, as General Manager of the District, have been instructed
by the Board of Directors to cooperate in any way with your
office, and to provide all requested information, in an effort
to resolve this issue.

It is not the wish nor the intent of the District Directors or
personnel to remain in a conflicting position. We have taken,
and still maintain, a position of no wrong doing to this date.

If you wish to acquire additional information, copies of contracts
or correspondence, please do not hesitate to contact the District
office to make your requests.

Sincerely,

Michael B. Kenny
General Manager

Mike Kenny, Manager
Shasta Dam Area, P.U.D.

Re: GC-81/0706

Dear Mr. Kenny:

The intent of this letter is to confirm our telephone
conversation on October 7, 1981.

My understanding was that you would provide me with
information concerning Project 8101 and an outline of your
procedures for negotiating contracts of a similiar nature.

I would appreciate receiving the above information at
your earliest convenience, but may I ask that it be no later
than October 20, 1981.

Thank you in advance for your cooperation in this matter.

Very truly yours,

Mark D. Walker
Investigator II
Enforcement Division

December 28, 1981


Mr. Chriss
Acting General Manager
Shasta Dam Public Utility Dist.
1650 Stanton Drive
Central Valley, CA  96019

Dear Mr. Chriss:

     As I indicated in my recent telephone conversation with
you, the Legal Division of the Fair Political Practices
Commission is currently considering a question presented to
it by your predecessor, Mr. Kenny.  Mr. Kenny requested
that we determine whether the Shasta Dam Public Utility
District must include Mr. W. A. Gelonek, who performs pro-
fessional engineering services for the District, as a designated
consultant in their Conflicts of Interest Code.  As we discussed,
you should receive an answer to this question by approximately
January 15, 1982.

     Once you have had an opportunity to review the District's
file on this matter, please feel free to contact me at (916)
322-5901, if you have any questions.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Janis Shank McClean
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

December 28, 1981


Mr. Collin Hammans
P.O. Box 492
Project City, CA  96079

Dear Mr. Hammans:

     I wanted to advise you that Mr. Chriss, the
Acting General Manager of the Shasta Dam Public
Utility District, and I have agreed to extend the
completion date for the advice letter on the W. A.
Gelonek question from December 21, 1981, to approximately January 15, 1982. You will receive a copy
of the letter as soon as it is completed.

                                Very truly yours,

                                [signature]

December 28, 1981


Mr. William Coyle
Box 793
Project City, CA 96079

Dear Mr. Coyle:

     Thank you for your letter of December 5, 1981. I appre-
ciated receiving your comments and observations on the question
of whether the Shasta Dam Public Utility District must include
Mr. W. A. Galonek as a designated consultant in the District's
Conflict of Interests Code.

     Due to my need for additional time in which to prepare
a reply, I have contacted Mr. Chriss, the Acting General
Manager of the District, and he has agreed to extend the
previously agreed upon response date from December 21, 1981,
to approximately January 15, 1981.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        [signature]
                                        Janis Shank McLean
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

JSM:km

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-4441 322-6444

November 25, 1981


William Coyle
Box 793
Project City, CA 96079

Dear Mr. Coyle:

     I am writing in response to your request for information
from this office. As you are aware, the Fair Political
Practices Commission has been asked by Mr. Michael Kenny,
General Manager of the Shasta Dam Public Utility District
(District), to consider whether the District must include
Mr. W. A. Gelonek, who performs professional engineering
services for the District, as a designated consultant in
their Conflict of Interest Code. Designated consultants are
subject to the disclosure and disqualification provisions of
the Political Reform Act.

     Government Code Section 83114 (see enclosure #1), empowers
the Commission to render such advice, and a reply is currently
being prepared. If you have any information that may be
relevant in formulating our reply, we would be happy to
consider it. You should forward any such material to us by
December 7, 1981. We will issue an answer to Mr. Kenny two
weeks from that date.

     In order to assist you in deciding what information may
be relevant to our determination of this question, I am
summarizing the issues which have been presented by Mr. Kenny.
The Political Reform Act prohibits a local "public official"
from making, participating in making or in any way attempting
to use his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest (see enclosure #2). The term "public
official" includes consultants to local governments who
satisfy the definition provided by 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18700(a)(2) (see enclosure #3). Consultants within this
definition must be designated in their employer-agency's
conflict of interest code. Please note, however, that 2 Cal.

William Coyle
November 25, 1981
Page Two


Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(2)(A) and (B) provides for certain
types of consultants who are specifically exempt from the
Act's coverage.

     In the case of Mr. Gelonek, the first issue is to determine
whether the professional engineering services he performs
for the District are such as to make him a consultant within
the meaning of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(2). If so,
he should be a designated consultant in the District's Conflict
of Interest Code.

     If Mr. Gelonek is a "consultant" as defined in the Act,
the second question is what type of financial disclosure or
disqualification should be required of him under the District's
Conflict of Interest Code.

     These issues are the only ones the Commission will
address.

     In answer to the specific questions raised in your
letter:

     (1)  The Chairman of the Fair Political Practices Commission
          is Mr. Thomas Houston.  The other Commission members
          are Mr. Art Metzger; Ms. Colleen McAndrews; Ms. Mickey
          Ziffren; and, Mr. Charles Conrad.

     (2)  All records pertaining to ongoing investigations
          conducted by this office are confidential, pursuant
          to Government Code Section 6254(f), and so I am
          unable to satisfy your request for a copy of our
          file or of any investigation reports (see enclosure
          #4).

     (3)  Under the provisions of Government Code Section
          6254(a) (see enclosure #4), preliminary drafts,
          notes or interagency memoranda are confidential.
          This includes such things as notes of telephone
          conversations.  Thus, I cannot provide you with
          these items as you requested.

William Coyle
November 25, 1981
Page Three


     Thank you for contacting me on this matter. Again, if
you have any relevant material that you feel would help the
Commission in making its determination on Mr. Kenny's request
for advice, please forward it to me by December 7, 1981.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Janis Shank McLean
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

JSM:plh
Enclosures

embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-5441 322-5644 February 8, 1982 Ron Criss Acting General Manager Shasta Dam Area Public Utility District 1650 Stanton Drive Central Valley, CA 96019 Dear Mr. Criss: Thank you for sending the information that I received January 22, 1982; this material has answered the remaining questions I had. As you know, your predecessor, Mr. Michael Kenny, asked the Fair Political Practices Commission whether the Shasta Dam Public Utility District (District) must include Mr. W. A. Gelonek, who performs professional engineering services for the District, as a designated consultant in its Conflict of Interest Code. You have indicated a continuing interest in this question. As I understand the situation, during the last year,1/ Mr. Gelonek and his firm, Gelonek and Associates, have performed work for the District under two types of contracts. In the majority of cases, the firm was employed to work on specific engineering projects, such as the preparation of a report or study of the design of a particular District sewage treatment plant or other project. (Examples of this are Contract #8730, "Sewage Treatment Plant Expansion," and Contract #8028, "Continundi Site Study.") The other type of contract entered into was for "General Engineering Services." Under the contracts labeled "General Engineering Services" (Contracts #8101 and 8002), Mr. Gelonek appears to have performed the type of activities that would be performed by _______________________________ 1/ For purposes of this letter, I will focus on the work Mr. Gelonek performed for the District during 1980-1981, although I am aware that he performed engineering services for the District prior to this time. Ron Criss February 8, 1982 Page Two a full-time staff engineer in a larger district: he reviewed and updated the time schedule for District projects; he participated in discussions regarding District personnel and needs (specifically the review of the Engineering Tech I position needs of the District); he was involved in the preliminary estimates and design of possible future projects; he prepared and presented reports to the Board; he prepared letters on sewer requirements; and he met with District personnel to discuss technical matters. The initial question we were asked was whether the position of engineering consultant must be designated in the District's Conflict of Interest Code. The Political Reform Act requires consultants to local governmental entities to be designated in the entity's code if the consultant makes or participates in the making of decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest. (Government Code Section 87302.2/ copy enclosed.) A "consultant," within the meaning of the Act, includes "any natural person who provides, under contract, information, advice, recommendation or counsel to a local government agency ..." (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(2), copy enclosed). This term does not include, however, a person who: (A) Conducts research
